hints:
Tense: First Conditional.  
Formula: **If + Present Simple, will + V1**.  
Explanation: The main part of the first conditional requires will + base form of the verb.  
Example: *If we come home late, mum will be angry.*  
Markers: If + Present Simple, will + V1.

a1.is:
❌ "is" does not have the auxiliary will, and the result of the first conditional requires will + verb.  
Example: *If we come home late, mum will be angry.*
a1.won't be:
❌ "won't be" is not suitable because it is a negative form, while the sentence needs the affirmative "will be".  
Example: *If we come home late, mum will be angry.*
a1.will be:
✅ "will be" is correct because the result in the first conditional is expressed by the construction will + verb.  
Example: *If we come home late, mum will be angry.*

hints:
Tense: First Conditional.  
Formula: **If + Present Simple, won't + V1**.  
Explanation: In the negation of the main part, we place will not (won't) before the base form of the verb.  
Example: *He won't come to your party if you don't invite him.*  
Markers: If + Present Simple, will not + V1.

a1.will come:
❌ "will come" asserts that the action will happen, while we need the negation "won't come".  
Example: *He won't come to your party if you don't invite him.*
a1.comes:
❌ "comes" is used in Present Simple without will, while the negative result should have won't + verb.  
Example: *He won't come to your party if you don't invite him.*
a1.won't come:
✅ "won't come" is correct because for negation in the main part we place won't + verb.  
Example: *He won't come to your party if you don't invite him.*

hints:
Tense: First Conditional.  
Formula: **If + Present Simple, will + V1**.  
Explanation: The main part of the first conditional requires will + base form of the verb.  
Example: *If you make lunch, I will wash the dishes.*  
Markers: If + Present Simple, will + V1.

a1.wash:
❌ "wash" does not have the auxiliary will, and the result of the first conditional requires will + verb.  
Example: *If you make lunch, I will wash the dishes.*
a1.will wash:
✅ "will wash" is correct, as the result in the first conditional is expressed with the construction will + verb.  
Example: *If you make lunch, I will wash the dishes.*
a1.washes:
❌ "washes" does not convey the future result. You need to use the construction will + verb.  
Example: *If you make lunch, I will wash the dishes.*

hints:
Time: First Conditional.  
Formula: **If + Present Simple, + V1 (command)**.  
Explanation: The result can be in the form of a command, so we use the base form of the verb without will.  
Example: *If it rains, take an umbrella.*  
Markers: If + Present Simple, (just) base verb.

a1.take:
✅ "take" is correct, as after if the sentence presents the result as a command in the base form without will.  
Example: *If it rains, take an umbrella.*
a1.you will take:
❌ "you will take" contains will, but in the command form after if we only use the base verb.  
Example: *If it rains, take an umbrella.*
a1.will take:
❌ "will take" contains will, but in the command form after if we only use the base verb.  
Example: *If it rains, take an umbrella.*

hints:
Time: First Conditional.  
Formula: **If + Present Simple, + V1 (command)**.  
Explanation: The result can be in the form of a command, so we use the base form of the verb without will.  
Example: *If you want to come with us, call me.*  
Markers: If + Present Simple, (just) base verb.

a1.you call:
❌ After if, we form the command only with the base verb. Choose "call".  
Example: *If you want to come with us, call me.*
a1.call:
✅ "call" is correct, as after if the sentence presents the result as a command in the base form without will.  
Example: *If you want to come with us, call me.*
a1.called:
❌ "called" is in the past tense, and the command must be in the base form: "call".  
Example: *If you want to come with us, call me.*

hints:
Time: First Conditional.  
Formula: **If + Present Simple, + V1 (command)**.  
Explanation: The result can be in the form of a command, so we use the base form of the verb without will.  
Example: *If you can't find the answer, ask your teacher.*  
Markers: If + Present Simple, (just) base verb.

a1.asked:
❌ "asked" is in the past tense, and the command must be in the base form: "ask".  
Example: *If you can't find the answer, ask your teacher.*
a1.will ask:
❌ "will ask" contains will, but in the command form after if we only use the base verb.  
Example: *If you can't find the answer, ask your teacher.*
a1.ask:
✅ "ask" is correct, as after if the sentence presents the result as a command in the base form without will.  
Example: *If you can't find the answer, ask your teacher.*

hints:
Time: First Conditional.  
Formula: **If + Present Simple, will + V1**.  
Explanation: In the if-clause of the first conditional, we always use Present Simple without will.  
Example: *Meg will be ill if she eats a lot of chocolates.*  
Markers: If + Present Simple, will + V1.

a1.eats:
✅ "eats" is correct, as in the if-clause of the first conditional Present Simple is needed.  
Example: *Meg will be ill if she eats a lot of chocolates.*
a1.will eat:
❌ The conditional part requires Present Simple without will.  
Example: *Meg will be ill if she eats a lot of chocolates.*
a1.eat:
❌ The conditional part requires Present Simple without will.  
Example: *Meg will be ill if she eats a lot of chocolates.*

hints:
Time: First Conditional.  
Formula: **If + Present Simple, will + V1**.  
Explanation: In the if-clause of the first conditional, we always use Present Simple without will.  
Example: *The boys will have to play well if they want to win the game.*  
Markers: If + Present Simple, will + V1.

a1.wanted:
❌ The conditional part requires Present Simple without will.  
Example: *The boys will have to play well if they want to win the game.*
a1.will want:
❌ The conditional part requires Present Simple without will.  
Example: *The boys will have to play well if they want to win the game.*
a1.want:
✅ "want" is correct because Present Simple is needed in the if-clause of the first conditional.  
Example: *The boys will have to play well if they want to win the game.*

hints:
Tense: First Conditional.  
Formula: **If + Present Simple, will + V1**.  
Explanation: In the if-clause of the first conditional, we always use Present Simple without will.  
Example: *If it snows, we'll go skiing.*  
Markers: If + Present Simple, will + V1.

a1.snow:
❌ The conditional part requires Present Simple without will.  
Example: *If it snows, we'll go skiing.*
a1.will snow:
❌ "will snow" has the wrong ending or will. The conditional part must be in Present Simple: "snows".  
Example: *If it snows, we'll go skiing.*
a1.snows:
✅ "snows" is correct because Present Simple is needed in the if-clause of the first conditional.  
Example: *If it snows, we'll go skiing.*

hints:
Tense: First Conditional.  
Formula: **If + Present Simple, will + V1**.  
Explanation: In the if-clause of the first conditional, we always use Present Simple without will.  
Example: *If you stay up late, you'll be tired in the morning.*  
Markers: If + Present Simple, will + V1.

a1.'ll stay:
❌ "'ll stay" contains the contracted will, but in the if-clause of the first conditional we only use Present Simple.  
Example: *If you stay up late, you'll be tired in the morning.*
a1.stay:
✅ "stay" is correct because Present Simple is needed in the if-clause of the first conditional.  
Example: *If you stay up late, you'll be tired in the morning.*
a1.stays:
❌ "stays" has the wrong ending or will. The conditional part must be in Present Simple: "stay".  
Example: *If you stay up late, you'll be tired in the morning.*

hints:
Tense: First Conditional.  
Formula: **If + do/does not + V1, will + V1**.  
Explanation: Negation in the if-clause is built using do/does + not and the base form of the verb.  
Example: *If he doesn't do his homework, his teacher won't be pleased.*  
Markers: If + do/does not + V1, will + V1.

a1.don't do:
❌ "don't do" uses do not, but with the third person singular, you need does not.  
Example: *If he doesn't do his homework, his teacher won't be pleased.*
a1.does:
❌ "does" does not contain the correct base form after does/doesn't. It should be "doesn't do".  
Example: *If he doesn't do his homework, his teacher won't be pleased.*
a1.doesn't do:
✅ "doesn't do" is correct because we build the negative if-clause using does not + base verb.  
Example: *If he doesn't do his homework, his teacher won't be pleased.*

hints:
Tense: First Conditional.  
Formula: **If + do/does not + V1, will + V1**.  
Explanation: Negation in the if-clause is built using do/does + not and the base form of the verb.  
Example: *If he doesn't pass all his exams, his father won't buy him a bike.*  
Markers: If + do/does not + V1, will + V1.

a1.don't pass:
❌ "don't pass" uses do not, but with the third person singular, you need does not.  
Example: *If he doesn't pass all his exams, his father won't buy him a bike.*
a1.doesn't passes:
❌ "doesn't passes" does not contain the correct base form after does/doesn't. It should be "doesn't pass".  
Example: *If he doesn't pass all his exams, his father won't buy him a bike.*
a1.doesn't pass:
✅ "doesn't pass" is correct because we build the negative if-clause using does not + base verb.  
Example: *If he doesn't pass all his exams, his father won't buy him a bike.*
